title: Chapter 37: The Competency
content: Three weeks after the show, the Palais de Justice smelled of old wood and older authority.

Jane sat in the gallery's third row, wedged between Richard and a French journalist who kept checking her phone as though the proceedings were merely a prelude to a better story. The courtroom was packed — standing room only, cameras clustered at the back like a steel forest, their red recording lights winking in the dim fluorescents. Someone had smuggled in a sketch artist who drew furiously, charcoal flying across paper, capturing the scene for outlets that couldn't film inside. Helen's exposé had done what Helen's exposés always did: it had turned a private injustice into an international cause. #FreeBerthaMason had trended in fourteen countries. The courtroom's aged oak paneling seemed to lean inward, as if the building itself wanted to hear.

At the counsel table on the left, Edward's legal team sat four deep. Suits so tailored they looked poured on. Documents stacked with surgical precision. The lead lawyer, Maître Delacroix — silver-haired, silver-tongued, rumored to bill more per hour than most Parisians earned in a month — arranged his papers with the calm of a man who had won harder fights than this.
On the right, Richard sat with a single legal aide and a cardboard box of evidence. The asymmetry was almost comic.

The judge, Madame Présidienne Fontaine, entered. The room rose. She was a small woman with reading glasses perched on a sharp nose and a reputation for procedural rigor that made both sides uneasy. She settled into her chair, adjusted her glasses, and said, in French that the court translator rendered a half-beat behind: "We are here to consider the defense motion to dismiss the criminal case against Edward Fairfax Rochester on the grounds that the primary witness, Bertha Antoinetta Mason, has been declared mentally incompetent and cannot testify. Counsel, proceed."
Maître Delacroix rose. He had a voice like polished marble — smooth, expensive, seemingly impervious to doubt.

"Your Honor, the facts are not in dispute. Miss Mason was diagnosed in 2021 with bipolar II disorder with psychotic features by two licensed psychiatrists. She was placed under court-ordered treatment. She was subject to involuntary commitment in 2023 after an episode that presented a danger to herself and others. These are medical facts, documented and upheld on appeal."

He placed a hand on the stack of files. "Mr. Rochester, as Miss Mason's husband, acted within the bounds of the law to ensure her care. The commitment order was signed by a judge — not by him. The treatment plan was designed by doctors — not by him. His only role was that of a husband trying to protect a woman he loved from the ravages of her own mind."
The translator's voice was clinical, but Delacroix's delivery was anything but. He made it sound almost tender. Jane's hands curled in her lap.

"The current claims made by Miss Mason — that she was imprisoned, that her diagnosis was manipulated, that she was held against her will — are, with respect, textbook presentations of paranoid ideation consistent with her condition. Her refusal to accept her diagnosis is itself a symptom. The law recognized her incompetence to manage her own affairs. It follows that she is incompetent to testify credibly about them."

Delacroix called his first expert. Dr. Renaud, a psychiatrist from Hôpital Sainte-Anne, testified that he had reviewed Bertha's file — not examined her in person, just the file — and confirmed the diagnosis was sound. The second expert, Dr. Petit, had been Bertha's treating physician for eighteen months. He described her "episodes" in careful clinical language: the rages, the instability, the noncompliance with medication. He painted a portrait of a woman too ill to understand her own illness.
Jane watched Richard's face. He sat still as stone.
The judge asked Delacroix if he had further evidence. He did not.
"Opposing counsel?" Madame Fontaine said.

Richard stood. He was not a lawyer. He was Bertha's brother, a man who had spent three years searching for a sister he'd been told was "receiving treatment abroad," and another two years fighting a legal system that had already decided she was incompetent before she could speak for herself. His French was serviceable but accented. His hands trembled slightly as he placed a laptop on the evidence table.
"Your Honor, I would like to present recordings made by my sister between 2021 and 2026."

Delacroix was on his feet. "Objection. Recordings made by a person under a competency order have no legal standing. She was declared unable to manage her own affairs — which includes the capacity to create admissible evidence."
The objection landed like a gavel. Madame Fontaine's pen hovered.

"Sustained, provisionally," she said. "The competency order is precisely what we are here to evaluate. I will hear the recordings in the context of determining whether that order should stand. Their evidentiary weight will be assessed accordingly."
Richard pressed play.
Bertha's voice filled the courtroom. Clear. Measured. Controlled.

"March 14th, 2022. I've been in the basement room for six weeks now. Edward says it's for my own safety. There's a lock on the outside of the door. I asked Grace if I could have a key for the inside. She said she'd ask. She never came back with an answer."

The recording continued. Entry after entry. Bertha describing her days — the routines she'd built to stay sane, the books she'd requested and received, the ones she'd requested and been denied. The way Grace brought meals on a schedule so rigid it felt like a prison timetable. The way Edward visited when he needed her signature on a document and left when he had it.

"September 3rd, 2023. They increased my medication today. I told Dr. Fournier I didn't need the higher dose. He said my 'noncompliance' was evidence of deterioration. I said I wanted a second opinion. He said that was also evidence of deterioration. I am beginning to understand that anything I say or do can be interpreted as a symptom. Including this recording."
A murmur rippled through the gallery. The sketch artist's charcoal never stopped.

Delacroix objected again — and again, Madame Fontaine allowed the recordings to continue, her pen making notes in a small, precise hand. Richard played six entries spanning five years. Each one was lucid. Each one was self-aware. Each one described, in Bertha's own calm voice, the mechanism by which her autonomy had been systematically dismantled.
The last recording was dated two weeks before the fashion show.

"I found a way out. Jane Eyre found the HELP codes in the collection. I don't know how. I don't know why. But someone is looking. Someone is listening. For the first time in five years, I have reason to believe this might end."
The courtroom was silent when the recording stopped. Delacroix's objection hung in the air, technically correct, legally sound — and utterly hollow against the voice they had all just heard.
"The objection is noted," Madame Fontaine said. "The recordings will be entered into the record pending the outcome of this hearing. Does opposing counsel wish to call witnesses?"
Richard looked at Jane.
She was already standing.

The bailiff swore her in. Jane settled into the witness chair, her palms flat on the armrests to hide the shaking. The courtroom lights were harsh overhead, washing everything into a flat, institutional pallor. She could smell the faint chemical residue of cleaning product on the wooden railing in front of her.
"State your name for the record."
"Jane Eyre."
"Your relationship to the defendant?"
"I was employed by Maison Rochester as a pattern cutter from June 2025 to February 2026."
"And your current relationship to the witness, Bertha Mason?"

Jane met Bertha's eyes across the room. Bertha sat beside Richard, hands folded, face composed — the same composure that had held her together through five years of locked doors and escalating sedation.
"I found her," Jane said. "In the basement of the atelier. Behind a door with a lock on the outside."
Delacroix rose for cross-examination. "Miss Eyre, you are not a psychiatrist. You are not a legal expert. You are not qualified to assess Miss Mason's competence."
"No," Jane said. "I'm a witness. I'm here to tell you what I saw."
"Then tell us what you saw."

Jane took a breath. The air tasted stale, recycled through decades of ventilation that had never been designed for this many bodies. She could hear the scratch of the sketch artist's charcoal, the hum of the fluorescent tubes, the barely audible click of a camera shutter from the press section.

"I saw HELP codes stitched into the linings of twelve garments in the Autumn-Winter collection. Morse code, hidden in the pattern matching. I traced them. They led me to the basement level of the atelier, to a room that does not appear on any floor plan filed with the building's permits. Inside that room, I found a woman who had been living there for over four years."
"Living — or receiving care?" Delacroix cut in.

"Living," Jane said. "Alone. Under lock and key. With a scheduled routine indistinguishable from solitary confinement. I found her financial records — accounts drained through a Seychelles-registered holding company that was also making monthly payments to Dr. Fournier. I found correspondence between Edward Rochester and his legal counsel discussing strategies to extend the competency order. I found a system, Maître Delacroix. Not a treatment plan. A system designed to keep a woman invisible."
Delacroix's smile tightened. "You're describing a conspiracy."
"I'm describing what I found."
"A woman diagnosed with a serious mental illness, whose own brother didn't know where she was for three years, was hiding in a basement — and you believe this supports her competence?"
"I believe the fact that she survived it does."
The courtroom rustled. Madame Fontaine raised a hand, and it stilled.
Delacroix tried another angle. "Miss Eyre, you were briefly romantically involved with my client. Is it not possible that your testimony is motivated by—"

"By what?" Jane's voice was quiet, but it carried. "By the fact that he lied to me? Yes. By the fact that he hid his wife in a basement while he pursued me? Yes. By the fact that when I found her, I understood that everything I thought I knew about my own life was built on a foundation of someone else's imprisonment? Yes. I am motivated, Maître. That is not the same as being dishonest."
Something shifted in the room. The journalists stopped typing. The judge's pen paused.
Madame Fontaine leaned forward. "Miss Eyre. You described a system. Are you claiming the entire medical and legal apparatus surrounding Miss Mason's case was fabricated?"
Jane weighed the word. Fabricated implied chaos, errors, a grand conspiracy sloppily assembled. What she had found was none of those things.

"Not fabricated," she said. "Weaponized. The diagnosis is real — I'm not qualified to say otherwise, and I wouldn't try. But the escalation was designed. The commitment was strategic. The payments from the Seychelles company to Dr. Fournier — twelve thousand euros a month, escalating to eighteen thousand after the 2023 commitment order — suggest that Dr. Fournier's clinical judgment had a financial supplement. The competency order was real. The way it was obtained and maintained was not."

She reached into the folder beside her and produced the bank records — the ones she'd spent three sleepless nights verifying with Richard's legal aide, tracing the wire transfers from a numbered account in Victoria, Mahé, through a shell company called Atelier Holdings Ltd., directly to Dr. Fournier's professional account.

Madame Fontaine took the documents. She read them slowly. Her reading glasses caught the overhead light as she looked up, and for a moment, Jane saw something change in the set of the judge's jaw — a tightening, a recognition. Not of the documents themselves, but of what they meant when placed beside a woman's voice, calm and precise, describing her own erasure.
Maître Delacroix requested a recess to prepare a response to the financial documents. Madame Fontaine denied it.

"The court has heard extensive testimony regarding the competence of the witness," she said, her voice carrying the weight of a system accustomed to moving slowly, now forced to move at all. "I am not prepared to make a final determination today. However — given the content of the recordings, which demonstrate a degree of lucidity and self-awareness that contradicts the description of paranoid ideation presented by the defense, and given the financial documents presented by Miss Eyre, which raise substantial questions about the independence of the medical opinions upon which the competency order was based — I am granting a temporary stay on the competency order, pending independent psychiatric evaluation by a court-appointed expert not previously involved in Miss Mason's care."
The gavel fell. The sound rang through the courtroom like a crack in a wall.

The steps of the Palais de Justice were cold beneath Jane's feet, the spring sunlight offering brightness without warmth. The crowd outside was larger than she'd expected — journalists, supporters, the curious — held behind barricades by gendarmes who looked as though they'd rather be anywhere else. Camera flashes strobed against the building's limestone façade.

Bertha emerged from the doors behind her. Richard had his arm through his sister's, guiding her through the press of bodies, but Bertha's step was steady. Her face, so long hidden, was now projected across a dozen screens held up by strangers. She didn't flinch.
"We won?" Bertha asked, her voice barely audible over the shouting of the reporters.
Jane turned. "We got a stay. Not the same as winning."

Bertha almost smiled. The expression was unfamiliar on her face — not because she hadn't smiled in five years, but because the kind of smile that wanted to form required muscles that had atrophied from disuse. It arrived crooked and unsure of itself, like a word in a language she'd once spoken fluently.
"It's the same as not losing," Bertha said. "For the first time in five years, not losing feels like winning."

The cameras found them — two women standing on the steps of the Palais de Justice, one who had searched and one who had been found, neither of them whole, both of them upright. A reporter shouted a question in rapid French. Another in English. A third held up a phone playing the clip that was already circling the globe: the courtroom recording, Bertha's voice saying *anything I say or do can be interpreted as a symptom*.

Jane looked at Bertha. The sunlight caught the silver threads in her dark hair, the fine lines around her eyes that five years of fluorescent basement lights had carved. She was standing in open air, in front of the world, and the competency order that had defined her as less than a person was, at least for today, suspended.
Inside the courthouse, behind doors that suddenly felt less impenetrable, Edward Rochester was watching his wall develop its first visible crack.
Outside, the cameras kept rolling.
